112080.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA believes that the executive board should be a good reflection of the city. It’s not just about men and women, but also about diverse qualities, cultural and ethnic backgrounds. This way, the board can make decisions that fit all residents better.

112081.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA thinks that citizens of Delft must always be able to participate in choices about the city. Not everything fits into a yes/no question, which is why we focus on good participation that suits the situation and lets everyone share their thoughts.

112084.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants everyone in Delft to live comfortably. That is why we give room to people who go out, but also take into account residents nearby. Together with entrepreneurs and residents, we ensure fair and good opening hours.

112086.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA believes everyone should be treated humanely, regardless of status. Shelter prevents distressing situations on the street and provides room for guidance and perspective. A humane city doesn’t drop anyone, especially not when things get difficult.

112090.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA sees that it is tremendously fun to experience a beautiful show on New Year’s Eve. Fireworks are bad for the environment and animals, so we prefer safe and animal-friendly alternatives, such as a light show with LED lighting.

112093.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA believes accessibility is important for everyone. We will talk with entrepreneurs, residents and other building managers to ensure clear rules. This way, the whole city will be accessible to all our residents.

112097.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA believes culture is important for the development of children. That is why we want all primary school children to be able to receive music lessons, regardless of their parents’ income. This requires extra money from the national government, which we will discuss.

112098.explanation: Good sports clubs not only lead to more people in Delft doing sports, but also to a friendly club where you get to know people. GroenLinks-PvdA therefore finds it crucial to support sports clubs.

112099.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA believes monument care is not a luxury, but an investment in our city. Proper maintenance prevents decay and high costs later. Heritage connects people, strengthens neighborhoods and contributes to a sustainable and future-proof city.

112100.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA deploys enforcement officers where human contact helps with compliance. Not everywhere are more enforcement officers necessary; sometimes the police are needed, otherwise neighborhood coaches and directors help make places pleasant and safe for everyone.

112101.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA believes camera surveillance is not always the best way to make a place safe. It can create a false sense of security and affects privacy. We only install cameras after a proper assessment. And if we do, then only European cameras.

112103.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA believes enforcement officers in Delft should not be given batons. This can provoke extra violence. We prefer to deploy enforcement officers where contact and explanation help to understand the rules and keep the city safe together.

112107.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA sees that there is hardly any vacancy in Delft (2%). This is not a solution for the severe housing shortage in our city and region. We want to explore how to effectively use the small amount of vacancy we do have.

112108.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA believes we urgently need solutions for student housing. Container homes help to reduce the pressure. Additionally, we work with housing corporations on new student homes with shared spaces for a pleasant and social living environment.

112109.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA prefers to work from trust. People apply for social assistance benefits for various reasons. Returning to work requires tailored solutions. The safety net in the Netherlands should truly be a safety net, and not linked to mandatory (social) work.

112110.explanation: Forgiving municipal taxes helps residents with low incomes. GroenLinks-PvdA sees that this helps residents make ends meet and avoid debt problems. This provides a much more pleasant life for these people and society.

112111.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA sees that people just above the social assistance level often do not get by. It is important to support this group. The municipality helps with this. The national government also plays a role, for example by raising the minimum wage and making the benefits system fairer.

112114.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA stimulates the mobility transition. We focus on electric driving and shared mobility. We ensure there are enough charging points and space for shared cars, bicycles, and mobility hubs.

112116.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA encourages people to go to shops on foot or by bike. But shopping must remain accessible for those with limited mobility. Therefore, we make an exception for people who truly need a car.

112117.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA considers the Delfthopper (demand-responsive bus) a good alternative for neighborhoods where regular buses do not run. This way everyone gets tailor-made transport to neighborhoods that are hard to reach.

112118.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA is happy with the existing environmental zone in the city center. We want to expand this. This way we keep polluting vehicles out of the center and ensure cleaner air and a healthier city for everyone.

112119.explanation: Public space is for everyone: for walking, cycling, playing, greenery, and parking. GroenLinks-PvdA wants to distribute the space fairly. We look at what is needed to keep each neighborhood pleasant to live in. Therefore, introducing parking permits in more neighborhoods can be a good solution.

112120.explanation: Everyone must receive the care they need. It is important that people feel safe and understood by their care provider. Therefore, we believe people should be able to choose a care provider that suits them. However, saving lives remains central.

112121.explanation: The municipality must primarily steer the quality of care for young people who need it most. We must support youth properly at the start of their lives. GroenLinks-PvdA wants the municipality to choose an organization best capable of providing this.

112124.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA Delft supports raising the tourist tax when necessary. This keeps a balance between tourism and livability. Tourists are welcome and can enjoy the city comfortably. In consultation with the sector, we monitor price and quality so that extra proceeds make the city more beautiful.

112125.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA uses Delft's scarce space smartly, balancing housing, working, greenery, and leisure. We choose future-oriented, sustainable companies that, together with the TU and others, contribute to an innovative and sustainable economy.

112126.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA sees that residents like shopping on Sundays. At the same time, we consider small entrepreneurs who find it hard to recruit staff. We also see that our economy increases inequality and environmental problems. Together with entrepreneurs, we focus on a fair, sustainable economy.

112127.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA believes refugees with a residence permit deserve priority for social rental housing. Out of compassion, we ensure everyone gets a decent home.

112129.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA believes Theater De Veste should be renewed. A sustainable and future-proof theater that is accessible to all Delft residents. Theater De Veste promotes cultural participation and supports local initiatives. The theater is a place the entire city can use.

112132.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants the municipality to accept new coffeeshops. This way we provide safe and clear places for users. At the same time, we invest in good education and prevention. We align with national pilots for regulation and legalization.

112135.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants living in Delft to remain affordable, safe, and pleasant. We ensure a good balance between students and other residents. Therefore, we oppose landlords renting out individual rooms. We build student housing with housing corporations and also encourage subletting.

112137.explanation: The city must remain livable and especially make pedestrians happy. Examples like Beestenmarkt, the Market, and Mekelpark show this is possible. Parking should mainly be on the city's edges. This creates more space for greenery, playing children, terraces, and other nice things.

112139.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants student housing to remain affordable and the city to be livable and vibrant. Therefore, we build new student homes in the city, but also on campus or in surrounding municipalities. Where possible, we combine students with other target groups.

112140.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA wants Delft to be an attractive place for all companies that contribute to society. We stand for a strong business climate where companies can grow. This creates jobs for both theoretical and practical education graduates.

112141.explanation: GroenLinks-PvdA is fully committed to the district heating network. We are already well on our way and want to continue. The heating network reduces CO₂ emissions and provides Delftenaren, especially in social rental neighborhoods, with a stable, affordable, and sustainable heat source.

112080.explanation: STIP strives for a diverse council and college of mayor and aldermen. Quality and expertise come first, with a good reflection of society (including gender, background, and age) as the principle, without using strict quotas.

112081.explanation: Delft already has a Referendum Regulation that can be used if desired. We do believe careful consideration is needed about which subjects we decide on, the trade-offs we make, and what we do with the outcomes. Careful decision-making is paramount.

112084.explanation: We allow 24-hour serving times in designated nightlife zones. In residential streets, quiet at night applies; liveliness where possible, silence where necessary. STIP envisions a city that breathes, with full terraces and atmospheric squares.

112086.explanation: People fleeing war and violence deserve a safe place. Delft takes its legal responsibility and offers asylum seekers humane shelter. We pay attention to safe living places for LGBTIQA+ asylum seekers and other groups needing extra protection.

112090.explanation: STIP wants everyone to enter the new year safely and happily. Therefore, we advocate for a national fireworks ban. Instead, Delft organizes an appropriate show: festive, sustainable, and without risks. This way we make New Year's Eve a moment of connection, not damage.

112093.explanation: We make Delft more accessible for everyone. This way, elderly people, visually impaired and blind people, and wheelchair users can move well through the city.

112097.explanation: We want to stimulate activities and talent development for young people. Culture is important, but broad subsidies for every child are very expensive. The municipality should focus on extra support for children from low-income families.

112098.explanation: Sports club buildings are made more sustainable and prepared for future growth. Subsidies are available for this and we make no distinction between sports clubs for youth, elderly, or students. This ensures a fair distribution of subsidies.

112099.explanation: Monuments give Delft its unique character and attract visitors. Preserving history is essential for an attractive city. STIP believes we should invest better now in good maintenance; deferred maintenance is many times more expensive in the long term.

112100.explanation: More enforcement is not always better. STIP wants enforcement to be targeted and proportional, focusing on high-impact crimes, undermining, safe neighborhoods, and digital security.

112101.explanation: Cameras are deployed only in extreme cases, with full transparency.

112103.explanation: The monopoly on violence is primarily with the police. STIP sees BOAs as hosts and enforcers who work de-escalating. Carrying weapons does not suit their role and can unnecessarily harden situations. We prefer to invest in good communication and short lines with the police.

112107.explanation: Vacancy for business space in Delft is extremely low. Start-ups and growing companies need sufficient affordable space. We are open to a combination of living and working so that beautiful ideas can arise.

112108.explanation: The housing shortage demands quick solutions. STIP wants the municipality to actively seek places quickly available for temporary housing, in Delft and the region.

112109.explanation: Work is the best medicine, but STIP believes in encouragement rather than obligation. We choose customization and removing barriers, so people can participate in the labor market from their own strength.

112110.explanation: By forgiving debts, we help people with a minimum income stay out of debt. Stopping can cause financial distress and eventually higher social costs for debt counseling. Prevention is better than cure.

112111.explanation: Raising the threshold costs enormous money that we can only spend once. We prefer to focus on structural solutions that help people leave social assistance.

112114.explanation: The transition to electric transport is essential for clean air and climate goals. STIP wants innovation to have space; by designating more charging points, we make sustainable driving possible for every Delftenaar. We also focus on shared transport, hubs, and strong public transport.

112116.explanation: The car gets a less prominent place in the city's streets. Street parking shifts to central parking spots such as underground or aboveground garages and park-and-ride locations on Delft’s edges. We make cycling, public transport, and shared mobility more attractive.

112117.explanation: STIP wants Delft to invest in full-fledged, reliable, and frequent public transport accessible to everyone. Tailored transport must never be an excuse to cut fixed lines that keep the city and region connected.

112118.explanation: We are moving step by step towards an emission-free city center with a predictable sector roadmap. Deliveries will be clean and consolidated. This keeps the center accessible for those who really need to be there, without extra hassle for entrepreneurs.

112119.explanation: Space in Delft is scarce and each square meter can only be used once. STIP therefore makes clear choices: we prioritize housing, greenery, and livability over cars. Smart and compact use of space is the only way to keep the city accessible and pleasant for the future.

112120.explanation: Good care requires trust and a good connection between patient and provider. STIP believes freedom of choice is essential; a care provider who understands the language, culture or beliefs contributes to faster recovery and better quality of care for all Delft residents.

112121.explanation: Youth care is tailored work; different needs require an individual approach. STIP believes quality and availability of specialized care should come first. Focusing on the lowest price overlooks the care for children who need unique help.

112124.explanation: We want to determine tourist tax together with the sector. A portion of the proceeds will go back to the sector to support the implementation of the vision on tourism.

112125.explanation: There is no room in Delft for extra business parks. Currently, there is a large shortage of space for many entrepreneurs; from startups to plumbers, painters and the local baker. STIP wants business collective buildings and to combine living and working.

112126.explanation: With joint initiatives and extended opening hours, we make the city center livelier together. STIP finds it important that Delft encourages more collaboration in the city center between entrepreneurs and the cultural sector.

112127.explanation: People in vulnerable positions, like others with urgent needs, have the right to safe housing. Guaranteeing accommodation for status holders is essential for their integration and stability.

112129.explanation: Theater de Veste will as soon as possible receive a future-proof location in or near the city center. Live performing arts and meetings are central, and there is space for night culture. Invest in a new city theater that is ready for the future and offers space to creators and cultural entrepreneurs.

112132.explanation: We accommodate our residents and Delft entrepreneurs by allowing a fifth and sixth permit for a coffeeshop. It is important that Delft residents can legally and safely purchase soft drugs.

112135.explanation: Have a spare room in your house? STIP wants to make it easier to take someone in. Therefore, STIP will simplify subletting, for example by removing the minimum square meter requirement and allowing cohabitation in studios.

112137.explanation: The car will have a less prominent place in the city streets. Street parking will shift to central parking locations. This creates more space for safe sidewalks, bicycle parking, and helps make the city greener.

112139.explanation: The shortage of student housing is large; STIP wants 3,500 student homes built before 2030. The municipality will take the lead with a strict schedule and faster decision-making. We will make firm, binding agreements with partners such as TU Delft and DUWO.

112140.explanation: Delft's economy is thriving with strong growth in jobs, more startups, and the opening of new business buildings. Delft has the potential to be the cradle of the region, with YES!Delft as the main supplier of startups, scale-ups and potential market leaders.

112141.explanation: STIP aims to be completely off natural gas by 2050. We will do this by working on the heat transition in multiple neighborhoods simultaneously. We stimulate appropriate technologies such as district heating networks.

112080.explanation: D66 finds it important that the city executive reflects Delft's population. This includes a balanced ratio of men and women.

112081.explanation: D66 wants Delft residents to have direct influence in city governance. A referendum is one of the means to ensure this.

112084.explanation: D66 wants a vibrant city and a pleasant living environment. Therefore, joint rules remain necessary to limit nuisance and insecurity and to maintain the balance between nightlife and living.

112086.explanation: Even without residency status, people should not end up on the streets. D66 chooses humanity. Currently, rejected asylum seekers are only accommodated in large cities (bed-bath-bread). We want to work with the national government and region to find a feasible solution with good guidance.

112090.explanation: A central fireworks show contributes to a safer New Year's Eve now that the national fireworks ban comes into effect.

112093.explanation: Accessibility is the foundation: everyone must be able to participate. Entrepreneurs are first to take action themselves, but the municipality can help remove barriers with advice and targeted support.

112097.explanation: D66 wants every child to have the chance to discover their talents. Through the Rich School Day and Youth Fund, we make music lessons and culture accessible, even for families with limited means.

112098.explanation: D66 wants the municipality to assist associations with advice, collaboration and smart use of facilities. We address the root cause of financial distress to ensure healthy clubs.

112099.explanation: Monuments and heritage are part of Delft's identity. We invest in preservation and accessibility, such as at Museum Prinsenhof, and cherish our historic cityscape.

112100.explanation: D66 wants the right mix: prevention and neighborhood officers first, and where necessary extra enforcement officers at risk locations. Enforcement officers are extra eyes and ears in the neighborhood and can relieve the police, especially at hotspots and around hospitality venues.

112101.explanation: For D66, cameras are a last resort: temporary, targeted and proportionate, with evaluation. We prefer neighborhood officers, prevention and protecting privacy.

112103.explanation: D66 does not want to arm enforcement officers; the monopoly on violence belongs to the police. We choose good cooperation, clear tasks and support without batons.

112107.explanation: Vacant buildings can quickly provide housing. D66 wants to speed up transformation; where needed, the municipality can help specifically (procedures/impulse), so opportunities are not missed.

112108.explanation: Students need housing now. Therefore, D66 wants to use temporary housing opportunities and vacant buildings, with clear agreements on end dates so no one suddenly loses their room.

112109.explanation: D66 helps people towards work. For those longer on welfare, realistic obligations are appropriate: a meaningful counter-performance, such as volunteering, tailored to someone's situation.

112110.explanation: D66 wants money not to be a barrier to participation. Exemptions and minimum arrangements prevent debts and stress and ensure people don't get into further trouble.

112111.explanation: We want to make arrangements simpler and more accessible. By softening limits, we prevent losing help if you earn a little more. This makes the system fairer and ensures that working more always pays off.

112114.explanation: Electric driving requires reliable charging, even without a private driveway. D66 wants more charging points on the street, smartly placed where demand is high, making sustainability feasible.

112116.explanation: Parking at the In De Hoven shopping center is free for the first hours. We want to keep it that way.

112117.explanation: D66 wants a comprehensive public transport network. Demand-responsive transport can be a smart addition where bus lines fall short, but only if it is reliable, accessible and well connected to regular public transport.

112118.explanation: Clean air and an attractive center go together. With an environmental zone, we exclude the most polluting vehicles and help entrepreneurs and residents with clear transition rules.

112119.explanation: Parking pressure requires fair distribution. With permits and customization, we keep parking spots available for residents and reduce search traffic, with attention to visitors and informal caregivers.

112120.explanation: Good care is person-centered. Where possible, there must be choice for an appropriate care provider, but 'always' is not realistic when resources are scarce. We aim for respectful, inclusive care and freedom of choice.

112121.explanation: Quality and continuity count in youth care. D66 wants municipalities to steer on proven effective help and collaboration, not on the lowest price, because that costs more later.

112124.explanation: Delft may ask visitors to contribute to managing a historic city. We increase the tourist tax appropriately for the region, but do so carefully, especially while national VAT changes for lodging remain uncertain.

112125.explanation: D66 wants space for entrepreneurs, but not by continuously creating new areas in our densely packed city. We densify and make existing locations more sustainable and combine living-working where possible.

112126.explanation: Delft already has extensive Sunday opening hours. D66 wants entrepreneurs and residents in each shopping area to make agreements about opening times, vibrancy, and livability.

112127.explanation: Status holders must be housed. A home is the foundation to integrate and participate. We contribute our fair share and build more affordable homes for everyone.

112129.explanation: Theatre De Veste is an important cultural pillar for Delft. We invest in renewal and sustainability so the theatre remains future-proof and accessible to all.

112132.explanation: We choose a realistic drug policy with good regulation of existing coffee shops. New coffee shops are not necessary if supply meets demand and nuisance remains limited.

112135.explanation: Room rental can help address scarcity, especially for students and starters. However, we monitor the balance per neighborhood with clear rules and tackle nuisance.

112137.explanation: More parking spots in the city center mean less space for greenery and staying areas. We are expanding the car-reduced inner city, reducing street parking, and directing parking to garages/P+R.

112139.explanation: D66 primarily wants to build more on campus: on our initiative, research has started into 1,500–2,500 extra student homes there. Additionally, we involve neighboring municipalities in the task; we have already started this during this period.

112140.explanation: D66 wants Delft to remain the place for innovation. Not just with arbitrary subsidies, but by investing in the ecosystem and space: support via e.g. Yes!Delft, good conditions for establishments, and sufficient growth space for scale-ups.

112141.explanation: The heating network is crucial to get Delft off natural gas. D66 wants to push forward, focusing on affordability, transparency, and choice where possible, so residents can heat sustainably and reliably.

112080.explanation: It’s not about man or woman but about the quality of the respective person.

112081.explanation: Involving residents in plans beforehand is always good.

112084.explanation: Delft has an alcohol-discouraging policy and you get unfair competition.

112086.explanation: They do not belong in Delft.

112090.explanation: Fine as an alternative to the fireworks ban.

112093.explanation: Because Delft is working on an inclusive city where everyone is equal.

112097.explanation: That doesn’t reach all children, but mainly the highly educated children.

112098.explanation: The municipality should do nothing but often maintains the accommodation anyway.

112099.explanation: Especially in a historic city like Delft, cutting back on monument care is not a good idea.

112100.explanation: Conditions must be created so they can do their job well. Authorities should be expanded.

112101.explanation: Camera surveillance is important to us. It works preventively and solves crimes.

112103.explanation: Then they will take on the role of police officer.

112107.explanation: Provided that the municipality owns the respective office.

112108.explanation: We believe that TU Delft must take responsibility.

112109.explanation: Provided that there are no medical restrictions.

112110.explanation: These residents already have it hard enough.

112111.explanation: It was recently raised to 125 percent of the minimum income.

112114.explanation: Due to the increase of these vehicles.

112116.explanation: That already happens at shopping center De Hoven Passage. Let Delft manage it first.

112117.explanation: A good example is the former BUD bus.

112118.explanation: Has already happened, unfortunately without public consultation. This comes at the expense of entrepreneurs in the inner city.

112119.explanation: We think residents should get a free permit and that visitors should pay.

112120.explanation: We have confidence in the aid services.

112121.explanation: Attention must be paid to quality.

112124.explanation: It has already been raised; the amount is already high enough.

112125.explanation: Delft has more need for housing.

112126.explanation: They are already allowed to be open every Sunday.

112127.explanation: They should end up on the waiting list just like everyone else.

112129.explanation: No money for this at the moment.

112135.explanation: That hospitality policy was banned a long time ago.

112137.explanation: There is no space in the city center.

112139.explanation: In recent years, TU Delft has already been sufficiently facilitated by the municipality by converting homes in the neighborhoods.

112140.explanation: We do not do that with entrepreneurs in the city either.

112141.explanation: The burdens on the residents are not yet known.

112080.explanation: The Delft VVD chooses a college based on qualifications and experience, not on forcing a specific male-female ratio. The best people for the job must be the starting point.

112081.explanation: The 39 municipal council members represent the residents of Delft. They are elected to consider all different interests so that every Delftenaar is heard. The Delft VVD supports this proportional representation. We do not support one-sided referenda.

112084.explanation: Entrepreneurs determine their own opening hours, including on Sundays. We do not impose restrictions so that companies can freely respond to the demand and needs of their customers.

112086.explanation: We do not provide shelter or facilities for rejected asylum seekers or illegal migrants. Those who truly flee war and violence are accommodated within national agreements. Delft fulfills its duty in this, but does not go beyond that.

112090.explanation: Fireworks are wonderful. However, the Delft VVD does not consider it the municipality's task to organize and finance a fireworks show with taxpayers’ money. The municipality should provide maximum space for private initiatives.

112093.explanation: Entrepreneurs are responsible for their own buildings. The municipality sets clear guidelines and cooperates, but is not (financially) responsible for entrepreneurs’ buildings.

112097.explanation: VAK promotes art and culture for everyone with music and theatre lessons. Those in need can get support through schemes like the Delftpas. This way, we help targeted, without subsidizing everything collectively.

112098.explanation: We support sports clubs that play a social role. But financial support from the Delft VVD is not a blank check. Clubs must first look at: their own income, cooperation, and more efficient use of facilities.

112099.explanation: The historic city center is Delft’s main attraction. We must take good care of it. We believe we must preserve this heritage.

112100.explanation: The Delft VVD wants more visible and decisive enforcement in Delft. Extra public surveillance officers are needed to tackle nuisance and crime and increase the feeling of safety on the streets, especially in places where residents currently feel unsafe.

112101.explanation: The Delft VVD is a strong supporter of camera surveillance in strategic locations where safety is at risk. Camera surveillance can contribute to crime prevention and the rapid identification of offenders.

112103.explanation: The VVD supports equipping public officers with batons because enforcers must be able to do their work safely. Aggression on the streets is increasing, and good equipment is not a luxury but a necessity. Those who demand safety must ensure public officers can defend themselves.

112107.explanation: Converting vacant offices can help address the housing shortage but is primarily the responsibility of owners and developers. The municipality must remove obstacles and speed up procedures, not routinely step in with subsidies. Public funds should be used targeted and wisely.

112108.explanation: The VVD encourages TU Delft to build more student housing on campus. For emergencies, the municipality can temporarily step in by, for example, providing flexible or container housing.

112109.explanation: A social assistance benefit should not be a dead end. Meaningful social work provides structure, prevents loneliness, and helps people quickly find a role and place in society again. Being meaningfully occupied is the best social provision.

112110.explanation: The Delft VVD believes that waiver of municipal taxes is not a structural solution for Delftenaren with low incomes. Instead, the municipality should offer targeted help, for example in finding work.

112111.explanation: Tackling poverty requires tailor-made solutions, not automatic extension. Help must reach those who truly need it, and working must pay off. The VVD chooses targeted support and quick guidance to work, not ever-increasing limits.

112114.explanation: There must be enough charging points for electric cars, but not at the expense of existing parking spaces. Sustainability must not become a disguised parking ban through the back door.

112116.explanation: The Delft VVD believes that where parking is free now, it should remain so. Parking near shopping centers is often a must for residents to do their shopping and important for entrepreneurs to attract customers. If there is no necessity, we consider paid parking there unnecessary.

112117.explanation: The Delfthopper is not a replacement for good public transport. Delft deserves strong tram and bus connections in all neighborhoods, with attention to affordability.

112118.explanation: The Delft VVD is against an environmental zone as a standard measure. It limits accessibility and burdens residents and entrepreneurs with costs. Sustainability only works if it is feasible, affordable, and offers freedom of choice, and only once alternatives are in place.

112119.explanation: We focus on affordable, practical, and fair parking. If a parking permit is not needed to regulate parking pressure, we believe it should not be introduced.

112120.explanation: For the VVD, the principle is equal treatment for everyone. Care providers are professionally trained, work according to medical standards and professional ethics, and provide care based on what someone needs—not on who someone is or what they believe in.

112121.explanation: Help is aimed at those who truly need it. No expensive trajectories for issues that are part of normal growing up. We critically evaluate results and stop help that does not work, so resources go to children and youth who really need it.

112124.explanation: Tourists are welcome and important for our local economy. Raising the tourist tax makes Delft more expensive and drives visitors away. Any increase should only be done if it also benefits the tourism sector.

112125.explanation: In Delft, we make room for entrepreneurship. We strengthen and modernize business parks, commit to sufficient power capacity, and improve accessibility.

112126.explanation: It is up to entrepreneurs themselves to decide whether and on which Sundays they want to open their business.

112127.explanation: Those who truly flee war and violence are accommodated within national agreements. Delft fulfills its duty, but does not go beyond that. Delftenaren have priority for social housing.

112129.explanation: The VVD believes that Theater De Veste in Delft must be renovated. The building is outdated and no longer meets modern requirements. It is also not accessible to everyone. Renovation will provide a safe, contemporary theater where everyone is welcome.

112132.explanation: Delft already has several coffeeshops; no new ones are needed. Unfortunately, there is regularly nuisance and incidents around coffeeshops. The Delft VVD advocates a strict approach to incidents and crime.

112135.explanation: Room rental can help alleviate the housing shortage, especially for students and starters. The VVD permits this, provided it is well regulated. So no nuisance, and protection of livability in neighborhoods.

112137.explanation: Parking spaces in the center are scarce. Both residents and visitors use parking spots in the center. The Delft VVD wants to realize additional parking facilities in and around the center so homes and shops remain easily accessible.

112139.explanation: We build more student housing on campus. Additionally, we encourage building student homes in cooperation with surrounding municipalities that are well accessible from Delft.

112140.explanation: The Delft VVD supports companies that bring innovation and employment to Delft. Financial support must be carefully considered. The municipality can also offer support by providing a good business climate, space, housing for employees, etc.

112141.explanation: The Delft VVD is against the automatic expansion of the district heating network. District heating networks are not an end in themselves but must be demonstrably affordable, reliable, and voluntary. Residents must always retain freedom of choice over their heating supply.

112080.explanation: Ideally, the executive board should reflect the population. However, this is not feasible and should not be a strict requirement. A year ago, there was only one man on the board; now, with Pechtold, there are two. That is not a problem at all.

112081.explanation: A good way for residents to have a municipal decision corrected. This also involves residents in decisions about their city.

112084.explanation: The municipality sets limits to protect neighbors and staff. However, there must be spaces in the city where nightlife venues can remain open longer.

112086.explanation: Rejected asylum seekers can be temporarily accommodated while preparing for their return. But not longer. It is not the municipality's role to obstruct other parties (churches) that do provide such shelter.

112090.explanation: Furthermore, more bonfires should be permitted again. Residents should also have space to organize their own activities.

112093.explanation: The municipality can assist with advice and permits. But entrepreneurs must pay for this themselves.

112097.explanation: This way, children are not dependent on their home situation to discover if making music suits them. Additionally, more places should be available for music lessons and rehearsal spaces for bands.

112098.explanation: Sports and exercise are good for people. They help prevent health issues and bring people together.

112099.explanation: Tourism and the historic city center belong to Delft.

112100.explanation: The enforcement officers assist with maintaining order in the city, adopt a coaching attitude, and are a valuable addition.

112101.explanation: Camera surveillance usually does not help increase safety. Active supervision by police officers or enforcement officers is far more effective. Cameras should be used sparingly and in specific cases. The SP opposes cameras or software from Chinese manufacturers.

112103.explanation: Enforcement officers also have a coaching and educational role, which becomes more difficult if they carry batons. Moreover, they are not trained or prepared to use force; that is reserved for the police.

112107.explanation: The municipality must enable the conversion of empty buildings into housing. If this is not feasible somewhere because it is too expensive, the municipality can help with subsidies. Additionally, 15,000 extra new homes must be built: 5,000 social rental and 5,000 affordable rental and owner-occupied homes.

112108.explanation: There isn't just a temporary shortage of student housing; there is a permanent one. Therefore, investments must be made in permanent student homes, including group housing. These should also be built on TU Delft land, which TU Delft has so far always opposed.

112109.explanation: When people can do socially meaningful work, they should be able to work and be offered suitable jobs with a decent salary. Only those who cannot work or cannot find work receive social assistance benefits. The SP opposes volunteers doing work that should actually be paid.

112110.explanation: The general rule underlying our tax system is that low incomes, including students, do not pay municipal taxes. Once the municipality stops this, more people will face problems, and the municipality will have to help them again. This costs more than it saves.

112111.explanation: Currently, mainly young families barely make ends meet on their income. As long as national politics does not solve this problem, it is better for the municipality to help these people immediately rather than wait until they are trapped in debt.

112114.explanation: There are currently people who cannot benefit from the advantages of electric driving because they do not have a charging spot.

112116.explanation: Furthermore, the municipality must make public transport, cycling, and walking in the city more attractive so that people who want to can choose these options naturally.

112117.explanation: People only structurally choose public transport if they can count on a fixed connection on a route. The municipality must invest in this. Demand-responsive transport is only suitable for a few specific cases.

112118.explanation: Reducing polluting vehicles should not only be done in the city center but everywhere. This is mainly a symbolic measure, also because most of the city center is already low-traffic. The SP wants to encourage businesses to make electric deliveries.

112119.explanation: Parking permits are needed in large parts of the city only because the municipality removes parking spaces. Coercion is not a good method to limit car use. Improving alternatives (public transport, cycling, walking) is a much better idea.

112120.explanation: Everyone should be able to choose their own healthcare provider. But the municipality does not have to ensure that a healthcare provider is available for every religion, culture, or belief.

112121.explanation: When it comes to protecting the most vulnerable group we have, children, we must choose quality and effectiveness. The municipality should not offer pointless care, as that only takes away resources from children who truly need help.

112124.explanation: That money should then be spent on making the city more attractive to tourists, especially to encourage more overnight stays in our beautiful city.

112125.explanation: There is no room for new industrial areas in Delft. These are better built in surrounding municipalities.

112126.explanation: The SP wants to support small, local shops. They often cannot be open every day. If large chains are open daily, these local shops risk disappearing. Everyone deserves a regular day off. Those who want to shop on Sunday can do so in the city center.

112127.explanation: Anyone who immediately needs housing or else will become homeless should be given priority for social rental housing.

112129.explanation: When building the theater, the cheapest option was chosen. As a result, the building is now outdated and difficult to maintain. We must not make that expensive mistake again. The city deserves a beautiful theater where various theater forms and music come into their own.

112132.explanation: If entrepreneurs believe there is demand for a new coffeeshop, it should be allowed, provided it meets all rules, i.e. not near schools. We should not pretend that soft drug use is illegal.

112135.explanation: This is already allowed, which is fine. However, no more family homes may be converted into individually rented rooms. At the same time, new student housing, including group homes, must be built—at least also on TU Delft land.

112137.explanation: Not in the center. But new parking spaces should be added around the center with good public transport connections to the center.

112139.explanation: Yes, new housing for young people should be built on TU ground. But new student housing is also welcome in other city areas. However, we do not want more family homes converted into student housing.

112140.explanation: Companies that have been around for a long time and cannot survive without municipal subsidies are not real companies. There are better ways to finance businesses. It does not matter whether a company is in Rijswijk, Delfgauw, or Delft. Municipalities should not waste money on that.

112141.explanation: We are lucky in Delft that the TU has tapped into an underground heat source. We must use this as effectively as possible to heat homes as cheaply as possible. And with the heat network, we spread the risks across municipalities.

112080.explanation: The CDA finds it important that men and women, people with disabilities, youth and elderly from all neighborhoods and districts are represented in our municipality, but that is not a goal in itself.

112081.explanation: The CDA is not in favor of a referendum. The municipal council is the people's representative and must sometimes make difficult trade-offs and decisions. However, it is important to involve residents well in important issues (in their living environment).

112084.explanation: The CDA values entrepreneurship, including in hospitality. Yet other interests and considerations naturally play a role. Therefore, the CDA wants the municipality and involved entrepreneurs’ associations to work together to reach an agreement.

112086.explanation: National policy states that rejected asylum seekers must leave the Netherlands. The CDA follows this line. If departure is not possible, mercy in distressing cases may be appropriate. We do not support making this municipal policy.

112090.explanation: Given the fireworks ban, this does not seem logical. The CDA favors exploring whether such activities can be organized in neighborhoods.

112093.explanation: The municipality has a task to make public spaces accessible. The CDA allocates funds for this. Keeping business buildings accessible is the responsibility of the businesses themselves.

112097.explanation: To develop those talents, it can be helpful if young people receive a push in the right direction. Because music has been proven to maintain and often improve mental health and social skills, the CDA has long advocated for extra funding for more music in the classroom.

112098.explanation: The CDA stands for a strong community life. Associations contribute to a strong society. Sports clubs that are struggling should therefore receive support from the municipality. This can also take the form of knowledge and expertise.

112099.explanation: We find it important to preserve local heritage. For the CDA, this includes religious heritage. That gives character and identity to our municipality.

112100.explanation: Municipal enforcement officers (boa’s) not only contribute to more safety in the city, but they are also a calling card. For that reason, it can be supportive to increase the number of boa’s.

112101.explanation: The CDA sees added value in camera surveillance to improve safety and wants it to be deployed when the situation calls for it. More camera surveillance is not a goal in itself, cannot replace manpower, and always requires careful consideration.

112103.explanation: The CDA is not fundamentally opposed to this, but would first like to wait for the results of the pilots currently taking place.

112107.explanation: When office space (unexpectedly) becomes vacant, the municipality takes an active role to prevent vacancy, for example by enabling the conversion of office space into housing.

112108.explanation: The municipality has already done this, and the flexible homes it now owns can be used for various groups of housing seekers, including students.

112109.explanation: The CDA wants to build a strong society. Everyone’s talents are needed for this and may be utilized.

112110.explanation: The municipality must show its human side: waiving municipal taxes is one of the most effective measures municipalities can take to support people with low incomes.

112111.explanation: Compared to neighboring municipalities, one could say that the current income limit in Delft is already on the high side. The CDA therefore finds it unwise to raise the income limit further.

112114.explanation: The number of charging points for electric cars must grow along with the number of electric cars. But it is a waste if we designate too many parking spots for this, as it reduces the spots available for the vast majority of cars.

112116.explanation: The CDA supports a tailored approach to paid parking. This means that some places require paid parking, while in others parking can be free. Often, entrepreneurs also offer to pay for the first or first three hours of parking for customers. A good thing!

112117.explanation: The municipality of Delft has no 'districts', but several residential neighborhoods that are not always easily accessible. The CDA favors full bus lines and is less in favor of on-demand buses like the Delfthopper.

112118.explanation: This is already policy in Delft (since January 1, 2025).

112119.explanation: Expansion of the paid parking zone only in consultation with local residents and entrepreneurs, and as much as possible tailored.

112120.explanation: The CDA values choice options in healthcare. Sometimes the differences in rates are so large or the quality so low that it is undesirable for every care provider to be funded by the municipality.

112121.explanation: The CDA focuses on normalization. This way, we not only approach young people more positively, but also ensure that those who truly need care receive excellent care. This movement should not only help us rely much more on talents but also save costs.

112124.explanation: Through the tourist tax, people from outside contribute to maintaining our facilities. The CDA would like the municipality and tourism sector to jointly come up with a widely supported proposal.

112125.explanation: There is little space for new industrial areas, and where space is still available, there is also demand for housing. The CDA prefers that the municipality does everything it can to retain current companies/industry and to stimulate business activity in neighborhoods.

112126.explanation: For the CDA, Sundays and public holidays count a bit less as working days than other days. Therefore, the CDA does not see the expansion of opening hours regulations as a given.

112127.explanation: Every municipality is obliged to shelter a number of status holders. Even after priority ends, Delft retains this legal obligation. Given the current housing shortage, the CDA is open to a discussion about balancing groups that also have priority for shelter.

112129.explanation: Renovating the current theatre is not a future-proof solution. In a few years, the city government must decide whether Delft wants demolition and new construction of a larger theatre or would prefer to invest the money in other matters. For the CDA, all options remain open.

112132.explanation: As far as the CDA is concerned, no new coffee shops will be added. Also, when a coffee shop closes, no new one will replace it.

112135.explanation: To reduce the housing shortage, the CDA definitely wants to explore how the existing housing stock can be better utilized. However, we think it is important that family homes remain as available as possible for families. They also belong in Delft.

112137.explanation: Expanding parking spaces in the city center is very difficult and comes at the expense of scarce space better used otherwise. The CDA does favor more parking spaces around the center, possibly an extra parking garage, or park-and-ride spots with a good public transport connection to the center.

112139.explanation: For new student housing projects, we also look at land around and belonging to TU Delft. Furthermore, smart use of space is a possible solution. Finally, Delft must continue to advocate strongly for the realization of student housing in the region.

112140.explanation: Companies founded on the campus sometimes grow so fast that they have to leave Delft. That is a natural development. Often, these companies establish themselves in the region and thus provide jobs to people living here. This also creates space for new companies.

112141.explanation: Whether we like it or not, in a few decades natural gas will no longer be available to heat homes. For some neighborhoods, a heating network is the best alternative. By smartly installing a heating network now, we are prepared for the future.

112080.explanation: For Onafhankelijk Delft, it matters whether someone is capable, responsible, and committed to Delft. The gender of the leader should never be decisive.

112081.explanation: Participation and having a say in major issues is a right that every resident should be able to use and be heard in.

112084.explanation: Of course, nuisance must be prevented, but Onafhankelijk Delft supports a lively city with the associated bustle.

112086.explanation: Onafhankelijk Delft believes that providing structural shelter to rejected asylum seekers is not a municipal task. After the asylum procedure is completed, the responsibility primarily lies with the national government.

112090.explanation: Agreed, due to the fireworks ban, the municipality must accommodate residents and provide a suitable location or locations for fireworks. Additionally, Onafhankelijk Delft supports permitting more bonfires and other initiatives.

112093.explanation: Making buildings accessible to people with disabilities is in the interest of the whole community. Therefore, targeted, voluntary subsidies or support from the municipality is defensible, provided it is used purposefully, temporarily, and transparently.

112097.explanation: Onafhankelijk Delft encourages that music lessons be given at every school.

112098.explanation: Onafhankelijk Delft believes there should be a possibility to help sports clubs if necessary, but not on a structural basis.

112099.explanation: To ensure safety, old municipal monuments must be maintained.

112100.explanation: Onafhankelijk Delft finds that boa’s often behave in an arrogant manner or are invisible. Boa’s should work more focused and not shy away from difficult situations.

112101.explanation: Provided that camera surveillance is justified and complies with privacy legislation and data protection law.

112103.explanation: Onafhankelijk Delft believes that the rights to carry a baton are only appropriate for police officers.

112107.explanation: Onafhankelijk Delft does not consider initiatives in this regard to be a municipal matter. Vacant properties must be addressed, with the possibility of fines.

112108.explanation: Onafhankelijk Delft believes that young people are being excluded and wants the same rights for students to apply to young people as well.

112110.explanation: Otherwise, people will get into even more trouble, which will cost the city more money.

112111.explanation: Even people with a middle income can struggle now that so many expenses have increased.

112114.explanation: Onafhankelijk Delft is concerned about the safety of nearby residents near charging stations because electric vehicle batteries can spontaneously catch fire. Therefore, Onafhankelijk Delft also does not want these in parking garages.

112116.explanation: However, the policy is not everywhere that parking is free. Onafhankelijk Delft does want the first two hours in every parking garage to be free.

112118.explanation: The policy has already been implemented. However, Onafhankelijk Delft wants this policy to be reversed. It has become a revenue model.

112119.explanation: Onafhankelijk Delft sees the parking permit policy as a cash cow. The argument of parking pressure is often incorrect but has led to the current policy. Onafhankelijk Delft wants to reverse the policy.

112120.explanation: Care must be available to everyone at all times.

112125.explanation: Onafhankelijk Delft does not see room for this and wants more affordable housing to be built on the scarce land.

112126.explanation: Onafhankelijk Delft believes it is up to the shopkeepers themselves to decide about shopping carts and opening hours.

112135.explanation: Because Onafhankelijk Delft is against subdividing houses into multiple student rooms.

112080.explanation: ChristenUnie advocates for a diverse council; a good balance between men and women is important, but other aspects can also play a role.

112081.explanation: Residents should feel heard by politics. Nevertheless, ChristenUnie does not see a referendum as the right approach. Political issues cannot be answered in isolation with a simple yes or no. Furthermore, every issue also requires attention to the voice of the minority.

112084.explanation: Although cafes and restaurants contribute to the coziness of the city, they can also cause nuisance to nearby residents. Therefore, when determining opening hours, the interests of the entrepreneur, residents, and visitors must all be taken into account.

112086.explanation: It is the responsibility of the Repatriation and Departure Service to return asylum seekers whose applications have been rejected to their country of origin. Until then, it is important that there is shelter for these people, as they are vulnerable on the street.

112090.explanation: A fireworks or light show brings residents together on New Year's Eve. We see a role for the residents themselves to organize an activity in the neighborhood. The municipality can support this.

112093.explanation: We commit to an accessible city. However, we see this as the responsibility of the entrepreneur themselves. The municipality can advise entrepreneurs on making their buildings accessible.

112097.explanation: Every child must have the chance to develop, including musically. Therefore, parents who cannot currently afford this should receive financial support. This can be done through the youth fund for sports and culture.

112098.explanation: The municipality must provide space for sports clubs and support them. However, it is not the municipality's role to financially support these clubs.

112099.explanation: Monuments are important for the history of our city. They remind us of the history of our city and that of the Netherlands. Therefore, we must maintain them well, for ourselves but also for future generations.

112100.explanation: A good balance between prevention and enforcement is important to ensure safety. Therefore, ChristenUnie wants to invest in preventive initiatives to reduce the workload on enforcement officers.

112101.explanation: ChristenUnie wants to allow camera surveillance only temporarily to increase safety at specific (unsafe) locations. We do not create safety with cameras, but with each other. We value privacy and therefore want to limit camera surveillance.

112103.explanation: ChristenUnie wants to increase safety through connections between people. In specific situations, preventive action may be necessary. Physical intervention is, according to ChristenUnie, a task for the police.

112107.explanation: ChristenUnie believes that converting vacant offices into homes should preferably be done without subsidies. We only want to use subsidies when it stimulates circularity. Additionally, the location of these offices determines whether conversion to housing is desirable.

112108.explanation: ChristenUnie considers adequate housing for students important. The preference is for permanent housing. When locations can only be temporarily developed, we still see this as an important opportunity to realize more student rooms in the short term.

112109.explanation: Participation in society is valuable for both individuals and society. As ChristenUnie, we find it important that people contribute to society according to their ability. This can also be voluntary work or informal care.

112110.explanation: ChristenUnie wants no one to live in poverty. Low incomes need exemption from charges. Stopping exemption increases social isolation of this group.

112111.explanation: Delft already uses a higher standard than social assistance in schemes for low incomes. However, we cannot help everyone in need of financial support. At the same time, we are aware that poverty schemes do not solve the core of poverty.

112114.explanation: ChristenUnie finds sustainability important and wants to encourage a switch to sustainable transport. Sufficient charging points are important; supply must follow demand.

112116.explanation: Accessibility of shopping centers is important, especially for less mobile residents. Therefore, ChristenUnie wants short-term parking to remain free. The primary responsibility lies with the shopping centers themselves.

112117.explanation: ChristenUnie wants all neighborhoods in Delft to be well connected by public transport. Then customization is not needed. Customization should never replace a timetable.

112118.explanation: ChristenUnie wants a car-reduced city center with sufficient parking facilities on the city outskirts. Delft already has a zero-emission zone for delivery vans and trucks in the city center.

112119.explanation: Whether you have to pay for a parking permit should not depend on your address. Additionally, expanding the permit system city-wide prevents a waterbed effect. This way, we ensure enough parking spaces remain available for neighborhood residents.

112120.explanation: ChristenUnie values freedom of choice. For collective provisions, it is not naturally possible to organize this, and therefore we do not want to take this as a starting point.

112121.explanation: ChristenUnie wants youth care to be accessible and to provide the right help quickly and adequately.

112124.explanation: Delft is a tourist city, and tourists use facilities in the city. ChristenUnie finds it reasonable for visitors to contribute a bit more, so that the costs for maintenance, cleaning, and facilities do not fall mostly on residents.

112125.explanation: Space in Delft is limited. ChristenUnie believes that available space should be handled carefully, with attention to housing, greenery, and livability. More intensive use of existing business parks is preferred, as is combining living and working.

112126.explanation: ChristenUnie sees the importance of a collective day of rest in our 24/7 society. Additionally, Sunday shopping puts pressure on employees and small entrepreneurs and does not necessarily contribute to a strong local economy.

112127.explanation: Status holders form a vulnerable group and have the right to housing. The municipality cannot fulfill its tasks otherwise. They must be able to integrate into society. At the same time, the Christian Union wants to invest in sufficient affordable housing for all home seekers.

112129.explanation: Theater De Veste is an important cultural facility for Delft. The Christian Union supports renewal, provided it is financially responsible for the city. Additionally, it is important that the renovated theater is accessible to everyone.

112132.explanation: The Christian Union wants to limit nuisance and health risks. More coffee shops do not contribute to this. The Christian Union wants fewer coffee shops and none within a 400-meter radius of secondary schools.

112135.explanation: House splitting can lead to nuisance and a decrease in livability in neighborhoods. However, the Christian Union wants to ensure that a home can be rented to a maximum of three people instead of two. This way, we make better use of the housing supply.

112137.explanation: Delft's city center benefits from fewer cars and more space for pedestrians, cyclists, and greenery. This contributes to a better and safer living environment. On the edges of the center, there must be sufficient parking spaces for residents.

112139.explanation: Students are part of the city. However, by building student housing on campus and in surrounding municipalities, the housing pressure can be better distributed.

112140.explanation: Delft is an incubator for innovative companies. These companies provide innovation and employment. The Christian Union wants to provide space for startups but does not want to financially support companies to stay in Delft.

112141.explanation: The energy transition is necessary to preserve our prosperity and become less dependent on geopolitics. The Christian Union supports the district heating network, provided it is reliable and affordable and residents are well involved in decisions about their energy supply.

112080.explanation: Volt believes it is important that public administration reflects the composition of society well. An equal number of men and women helps with this. Research also shows that teams with diverse backgrounds deliver better results.

112081.explanation: Volt wants residents to have input on important topics. A referendum offers too little room for nuance and cooperation. Volt prefers citizen assemblies or a combination of assemblies and referenda, so residents can have earlier, better, and joint influence on complex issues.

112084.explanation: Volt wants entrepreneurs to have the space to do business. At the same time, clear rules are needed so that hospitality venues and residents can together ensure a pleasant and livable city.

112086.explanation: Volt advocates for a fairer and faster asylum procedure and protecting human rights. Not every rejected asylum seeker can return and many are vulnerable to exploitation. In Delft, the municipality must comply with international treaties and provide basic services such as medical care.

112090.explanation: Volt sees a large fireworks display as an option, but it is expensive, polluting, and not accessible to everyone. Many people prefer to celebrate at home or with friends. Volt proposed a citizen assembly in Delft about New Year's Eve so that Delftenaren can think together about a safer and cleaner celebration.

112093.explanation: Volt believes that Delft should be accessible to everyone. Volt does not think the municipality should financially support initiatives to make businesses more accessible but does see a role for the municipality in encouraging and advising entrepreneurs.

112097.explanation: Volt commits to equal opportunities. Children and youth should be able to develop their talents and interests, also outside school hours. Initiatives like the "rich learning day," where schools, libraries, museums, and sports clubs collaborate, receive full support from Volt.

112098.explanation: Sports are healthy, and participating in sports clubs stimulates this. Municipal support can be provided but only in exceptional cases. Clubs facing difficulties should first seek alternative forms of funding themselves.

112099.explanation: Volt wants to preserve Delft's cultural-historical values and not cut funding for monument care. Monuments and historic buildings tell the story of our city and make Delft attractive for residents and tourists alike.

112100.explanation: Volt prefers to invest in prevention and community connection through well-being workers and youth workers to increase safety.

112101.explanation: Volt believes that more cameras usually do not solve problems but shift them. They only help when combined with better public space design, police, and youth work. Camera surveillance must always have a clear purpose and defined stopping agreements.

112103.explanation: Volt wants well-trained enforcement officers equipped with the right tools. Familiar faces in each neighborhood foster better local knowledge and reduce tensions. Greater visibility helps prevent violations. The baton is not a standard tool.

112107.explanation: Volt wants to convert vacant offices that are no longer suitable due to their location into housing. This helps to tackle the housing shortage. Because the land then becomes more valuable for developers, municipal subsidies are not really necessary.

112108.explanation: The shortage of student housing is growing, especially due to sales by private owners. Temporary housing provides quick additional space and enables renovations of existing student complexes.

112109.explanation: Volt chooses not to require a counter-performance from welfare recipients. The Netherlands is the only country in Europe that does this. Mandatory volunteer work does not always lead to suitable employment. Moreover, people may contribute better to society as a parent or caregiver.

112110.explanation: Volt believes that people with low incomes should continue to be able to apply for exemptions from municipal taxes. Preventing debts is cheaper than solving them; additionally, Volt focuses on education, early detection, and appropriate guidance.

112111.explanation: Volt thinks that people in poverty should receive good, accessible assistance. Help must reach everyone who needs it. In Delft, Volt ensured that the income limit for the energy allowance was raised so that working people with low incomes were also helped.

112114.explanation: Volt focuses on electric driving with sufficient charging stations and electric shared cars. This keeps cars available for those who need them, with less pollution and less space use. This aligns with the goal that Delft is climate-neutral by 2040.

112116.explanation: Volt primarily advocates for better accessibility of shopping centers by bicycle and public transport. Parking at shopping centers may be free for the first two hours for shopping purposes.

112117.explanation: The Delfthopper is a form of flexible transportation between Delft neighborhoods and surrounding villages. Volt believes the municipality should continue to offer this type of transport. It helps people stay mobile and can reduce loneliness.

112118.explanation: Volt supports banning polluting vehicles from the city center and backs the decision to make Delft's city center a zero-emission zone as of January 1, 2025. This decision supports Volt’s ambition for a climate-neutral Delft by 2040.

112119.explanation: Volt makes conscious choices regarding space use. Parking takes up a lot of space and reduces room for housing and greenery. Therefore, in neighborhoods with high parking pressure, Volt prefers to broaden parking permit requirements rather than create additional parking spaces.

112120.explanation: Volt finds it important that people feel comfortable with their healthcare provider, but even more important that people receive good care. Due to the current shortage of healthcare providers, it is not always possible to have a provider with a specific faith or belief.

112121.explanation: Volt wants good youth care without long waiting lists. The focus should be not on the lowest price but on the right and effective care, even if that is scarce or more expensive. Fewer providers can help organize care better and reduce waiting times.

112124.explanation: Volt thinks the tourist tax may be increased. The extra money can be used to support culture. That is good for tourism and for entrepreneurs in the city.

112125.explanation: Volt provides space for the economy, especially companies contributing to Delft's innovative ecosystem. Space for companies should be developed in combination with housing and from a regional perspective. Delft already has several business parks, but they can be better utilized.

112126.explanation: Volt wants entrepreneurs to keep the choice to open on Sundays or not. According to Volt, there is no reason to change the current policy in Delft.

112127.explanation: Volt wants Delft to fulfill its legal duty to house status holders. Without priority, this is virtually impossible. At the same time, we shorten waiting times for other home seekers by building more social rental homes so that more homes become available each year.

112129.explanation: Volt wants Delft to invest in culture. The current theater is not easily accessible and is not ready for the future. Therefore, Volt chooses a new, sustainable, and future-proof building. This building will provide space for all forms of performing arts and for social encounters.

112132.explanation: Volt is in favor of legalizing soft drugs, but until national decisions are made, we choose good regulation in Delft. Extra enforcement of traffic nuisance, better distribution of coffee shops, and openness to new locations reduce illegal trade and ensure a safer Delft.

112135.explanation: The rules for house splitting are currently too strict in Delft. Volt wants tailor-made approaches per neighborhood. In some neighborhoods, house splitting or dividing homes can help create more affordable housing. In others, it is more important to protect livability.

112137.explanation: Delft's city center must remain easily accessible. Volt prefers investing in good public transport and safe, attractive bicycle paths rather than encouraging car ownership or use. For residents of the inner city, there must be enough space in parking garages.

112139.explanation: Volt is pleased with the plans for student housing on the campus and in Rijswijk, as well as in the Kabeldistrict and around the Delft Campus station. For new projects, Volt examines which groups fit per location; students are not excluded in advance.

112140.explanation: Volt considers new, innovative companies important for the future of Delft, the Netherlands, and Europe. The municipality must, together with the region and province, ensure sufficient affordable business space, also when they expand.

112141.explanation: Volt wants the municipality to continue the construction of the district heating network. District heating networks are important for the transition to a green and sustainable energy system. The construction is disruptive to the neighborhood but also offers opportunities. Therefore, ideas from neighborhood residents should be collected and taken into account.

112080.explanation: The composition of the executive board should be based on quality and suitability, not on fixed allocations.

112081.explanation: Too often the executive board pushes through decisions that residents disagree with and cannot oppose. Therefore, the PVV wants a binding referendum so that residents have the final say on important decisions and projects not yet implemented.

112084.explanation: Entrepreneurs should be able to determine their own opening hours. The municipality should only intervene in case of concrete nuisance.

112086.explanation: Rejected asylum seekers have no right to shelter. Those without residence rights must leave. PVV Delft opposes bed-bath-bread policies and pull factors.

112090.explanation: PVV Delft is against a fireworks ban and supports the preservation of the fireworks tradition. This tradition cannot be replaced by a municipal show. Safety is achieved through enforcement, not symbolic politics on a stage.

112093.explanation: Accessibility is important, but obliging entrepreneurs to provide financial support is not a municipal task. PVV Delft chooses personal responsibility and targeted solutions, not new subsidies and regulations.

112097.explanation: Music lessons are valuable but are not part of the municipality's core tasks.

112098.explanation: Sports clubs are important for health and social cohesion. According to PVV Delft, targeted and temporary support is possible if clubs find themselves in difficulty.

112099.explanation: PVV Delft wants to handle the city's historical heritage carefully. Monument care is about preservation and maintenance, not luxury or prestige. Budget cuts must never lead to the decay of monuments that make Delft what it is.

112100.explanation: Safety requires visible presence on the streets. PVV Delft wants more enforcement officers to act against nuisance and insecurity. No looking away, but enforcing. Direct and visible.

112101.explanation: Safety in public spaces is paramount. CCTV helps to prevent crime and violence and to apprehend perpetrators faster. Privacy is important but must not outweigh residents' sense of safety and security in their own city.

112103.explanation: Enforcement officers involved in safety must be able to protect themselves. A baton is intended only as a means of protection, not for investigation. This applies solely to well-trained enforcement officers with a clear safety task.

112107.explanation: Building and converting homes is a task for housing corporations and market parties, not for the municipality. The municipality provides clear rules and speedy permits, not subsidies for buildings. Tax money is meant for safety, care, and facilities for residents.

112108.explanation: Student housing is the responsibility of the TU Delft, not the municipality. Temporary housing should be allocated to youth from Delft, not to students. Students belong on the campus.

112109.explanation: Those receiving social assistance benefits and able to participate should perform socially meaningful work. This increases self-worth and reduces dependency. PVV Delft chooses participation, not standing on the sidelines.

112110.explanation: PVV Delft wants to continue the remission of municipal taxes for people with low incomes. This arrangement prevents small charges from unnecessarily escalating into bigger problems. Abolishing it solves nothing and only puts extra pressure on those already struggling.

112111.explanation: Poverty is tackled by lowering burdens and making work pay, not by constantly adding new schemes and raising income limits.

112114.explanation: PVV Delft does not want to sacrifice extra parking spaces for charging stations. Parking spaces are scarce and must remain available for all cars. No preferential treatment for electric driving, but room for residents and entrepreneurs.

112116.explanation: Free parking at shopping centers is important for accessibility and liveliness. Visitors should easily be able to do their shopping without extra costs.

112117.explanation: Tailor-made transport between the town centers is needed, supplementing public transport. According to PVV Delft, this can be done via a call bus or community transport, with attention to seniors and low-income people.

112118.explanation: An environmental zone is symbolic policy that affects residents and entrepreneurs and yields little for livability. PVV Delft opts for accessibility and common sense.

112119.explanation: PVV Delft is against expanding parking permits. Where possible, PVV Delft wants to reverse permit parking. Residents should not have to pay to park their car in their own neighborhood.

112120.explanation: Care should focus on quality, availability, and safety. Not on religion or life philosophy. PVV Delft wants good care for everyone, without separate categories or exceptions.

112121.explanation: Youth care is about quality and continuity, not the lowest price. PVV Delft wants children to get the right help, on time and well. Budget cuts in youth care by shopping on price always come at the expense of the child.

112124.explanation: PVV Delft opposes raising the tourist tax. Tourism is important for the local economy and hospitality industry. Extra tax drives visitors away and harms entrepreneurs, while Delft must remain attractive.

112125.explanation: PVV Delft does not want to develop new business parks but to better utilize and more smartly arrange existing parks instead of designating new areas. Employment is essential for a strong and livable city.

112126.explanation: Entrepreneurs and consumers should decide when they want to buy or sell. PVV Delft wants more shopping Sundays and less paternalism. Freedom for entrepreneurs, choice for residents.

112127.explanation: PVV Delft opposes priority for status holders in social housing. They must, like everyone else, join the waiting list. Delft youth can only register from age 18; PVV Delft wants this to be possible from age 16, so they build waiting time sooner.

112129.explanation: Renovation of Theater De Veste is possible, provided it leads to a widely accessible theater for all Delftenaren. Not an elitist prestige project, but a place with a varied program for a broad audience.

112132.explanation: PVV Delft does not want new coffeeshops. They bring extra nuisance and insecurity. The municipality must focus on livability and safety for residents.

112135.explanation: Room rental worsens the housing crisis and undermines livability. Family homes are meant for families, not to be split up. PVV Delft wants to restore calm and livability to neighborhoods, not further rooming houses.
